common_name: Rainier Cherry (Blushing Cherry, Yellow Sweet Cherry)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Rainier requires 700–800 chill hours and a pollinizer (Bing, Black Tartarian, or Lapins) for good fruit production in most situations.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Rainier is considered the premium sweet cherry cultivar — producing large, blush-yellow to golden-red fruit with creamy yellow flesh that is exceptionally sweet, almost honeylike, with delicate flavor considered superior to the firmer, darker varieties. It was developed at Washington State University in 1954 and named for Mount Rainier. The firm, thick skin makes Rainier more resistant to cracking than some sweet cherries, but the pale color means bird damage can be severe — netting is often necessary. Rainier requires 700–800 winter chill hours (hours below 45°F) to fruit reliably, limiting it to zones 5–8; in warmer climates, performance is inconsistent. A pollinizer (Bing, Black Tartarian, or Lapins cherry) planted within 50 feet is necessary for full crop production. Prune during dormancy to maintain an open-center or modified central-leader form. Bears fruit on spurs that persist for many years — avoid pruning productive spur wood.
sources: Washington State University Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Black Tartarian is self-unfruitful and requires a pollinizer; it is itself an excellent pollinizer for Bing and Rainier and is often planted as a pollinizer companion.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Black Tartarian is one of the oldest and most reliable sweet cherry cultivars in cultivation — an early-season cherry with large, dark mahogany-red to nearly black, heart-shaped fruit with sweet, rich, dark-red flesh. It ripens 1–2 weeks before Bing and is one of the first sweet cherries to mature in a given location. The fruit is softer than Bing or Rainier and does not store or ship as well, making it particularly suited to home garden production and immediate fresh consumption. Black Tartarian also serves as an excellent pollinizer for most other sweet cherry varieties. It was imported from Russia in the early 1800s and is one of the oldest sweet cherry cultivars still in commercial and home garden production. Trees are vigorous, relatively disease-resistant, and productive. Require 900+ chill hours; best suited to zones 5–8 in areas with reliable cold winters.
sources: Washington State University Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; North Star is self-fertile and does not require a pollinizer, making it ideal for smaller gardens with space for only one cherry tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; North Star is a naturally dwarf tart cherry developed at the University of Minnesota that reaches only 8–12 feet tall — significantly smaller than standard sour cherry trees — making it manageable for home gardens and usable on semi-dwarfing rootstocks for even smaller trees. The fruit is tart, dark-red, with a short stem that makes hand harvesting easier than Montmorency. It is self-fertile, needing no pollinizer, and is exceptional for its hardiness — surviving zone 3 winters reliably. Like all tart cherries, North Star fruit is used primarily for pies, jams, juices, and preserves rather than fresh eating, but the intense cherry flavor is prized in those applications. Trees bear heavily and begin producing within 2–3 years of planting. North Star is more disease-resistant than sweet cherries, requiring less intensive management. An excellent choice for the northern home orchard where sweet cherries may be unreliable.

common_name: Bartlett Pear (Williams' Bon Chrétien Pear, Williams Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Bartlett requires 600–800 chill hours and benefits from a pollinizer (Bosc, Anjou) for maximum production in most situations.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Bartlett (Williams' Bon Chrétien in Europe) is the most widely grown pear in North America and the world's most commercially important pear cultivar — the classic yellow, buttery, bell-shaped fruit that defines the word 'pear' in popular culture. The large, sweet, aromatic fruit with fine-grained, melting flesh ripens from late summer to early fall and is excellent fresh, canned, dried, and used in pies and preserves. Trees are moderately susceptible to fire blight (Erwinia amylovora), which can cause significant damage in warm, humid climates; planting on well-drained sites and applying copper during bloom reduces risk. Bartlett needs 600–800 chill hours and is best suited to zones 5–7 where winters reliably provide sufficient cold. For warmer zones 8–9, low-chill pears like 'Hood', 'Flordahome', and 'Orient' are more appropriate than Bartlett. Trees bear at 3–5 years and live for decades.
sources: Washington State University Tree Fruit Research and Extension Center

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Methley is self-fertile and does not require a pollinizer — an important advantage for small home orchards with space for only one plum tree. It requires only 250–350 chill hours, making it one of the best plum selections for zone 8–9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Methley is one of the best Japanese plum cultivars for warm climates — a highly productive, self-fertile tree requiring only 250–350 chill hours (hours below 45°F), making it one of the most reliable plum choices for zone 8–9 Gulf Coast gardens where most other plum cultivars fail due to insufficient winter cold. The medium-sized fruit has red-purple skin and distinctive red flesh with a sweet-tart, juicy flavor. Methley is an early-season plum that ripens in May or June in zone 8–9, which allows the harvest to be completed before summer heat and disease pressure peak. It is also an effective pollinizer for many other Japanese plum cultivars. Trees are vigorous and productive, beginning to bear 2–3 years after planting. Prune annually after harvest to maintain tree structure and control size. A consistent performer in the Gulf Coast South where it is one of the standard recommendations from Texas A&M AgriLife Extension.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter or early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Beauty is the earliest-ripening Japanese plum and pollinates well with Santa Rosa and Methley. It requires approximately 250–400 chill hours, suitable for zone 7–9.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Beauty is one of the earliest-ripening Japanese plum cultivars, producing medium-sized, bright scarlet-red to crimson fruit with red-streaked yellow flesh in late spring — often the first plum available from the orchard in a given season. The flesh is sweet and juicy with good flavor for fresh eating, canning, and jam production. Beauty is partially self-fertile but produces best crops with a pollinizer nearby (Santa Rosa, Methley, or other Japanese plums). Its low chill requirement (approximately 250–400 hours) makes it well-adapted to zone 7–9, where it performs more reliably than higher-chill European plum cultivars. Trees are moderately vigorous and somewhat susceptible to brown rot, which can be managed with timely fungicide applications and prompt removal of infected fruit. Beauty ripens over an extended period of 2–3 weeks, reducing harvest pressure compared to plums that ripen all at once.
